description: This figure presents two groups of engineering stress-strain curves of austenitic and austenite-based duplex Fe-Mn-Al-C low-density steels. The first subplot demonstrates the effect of Al and C contents: all tested samples are treated by 1000℃/1h solutionizing followed by water quenching, including 20Mn-10Al-1.5C, 20Mn-11Al-1.0C, 20Mn-10Al-1.0C, and 22Mn-xAl-0.6C (x=0,3,6) series. It shows that increasing Al content reduces yield strength, ultimate tensile strength and strain hardening rate, while the Al-free 22Mn-0Al-0.6C steel exhibits the highest strength among the 22Mn series. The second subplot shows the effect of κ'-carbide and B2 intermetallic phases: tested alloys include 28Mn-12Al-1.0C under water quenching, 550℃ aging for 5/46 min, 18Mn-10Al-0.9C-5Ni under 900℃ holding for 2/15 min, and 28Mn-10Al-1.0C water quenched. Longer aging time increases yield strength but reduces total elongation, the B2-containing Ni-alloyed steel shows ultrahigh strength, and 28Mn-10Al-1.0C reaches ~100% elongation. The figure confirms that composition and heat treatment (precipitation regulation) significantly tune the strength-ductility trade-off of this steel system, and high Al/C steels show unique low or even negative work hardening rate while retaining good ductility.
